title: Memory & Storage — Your Computer's Two Types of Remembering
key_insight: RAM is what the computer is working on right now. Storage is what it keeps forever.
body:
## Two Ways to Remember

Your brain has two kinds of memory:
- **Working memory** — what you're thinking about right now (limited, fast)
- **Long-term memory** — everything you've ever learned (large, slower to access)

Computers have the exact same split:

### RAM (Random Access Memory) — Working Memory

- Very fast to read and write
- Loses everything when power turns off
- Usually 8–64 GB in a laptop
- Holds what you're currently working on: open programs, data being processed

### Storage (SSD/HDD) — Long-term Memory

- Slower than RAM
- Keeps data forever (even without power)
- Usually 256 GB to several TB
- Holds your files, photos, installed programs, AI model weights

### How They Work Together

```
1. You open a photo editing app (Storage → RAM)
2. You load an image file (Storage → RAM)
3. You apply a filter (CPU works in RAM)
4. You save the file (RAM → Storage)
5. You close the app (RAM freed)
```

Everything the CPU works on must pass through RAM first. Storage is too slow for the CPU to use directly.

### AI Connection

An AI model's weights are stored on disk (Storage). When you run the model, the weights load into RAM (or GPU memory). This is why:
- A 7B model needs 14 GB of GPU RAM to run at fp16
- First-use of a model is slow (loading from disk)
- Subsequent uses are faster (already in RAM)
common_mistakes: Thinking more storage = faster computer. Storage holds data. RAM + CPU speed = computer speed.
Confusing 'my computer is running out of memory' (RAM) with 'my disk is full' (storage) — very different problems
Forgetting that GPU VRAM is also RAM — it's just RAM on the graphics card, separate from system RAM
mini_exercise: Open your computer's task manager or Activity Monitor. Find how much RAM is being used right now vs how much is free. Then find how much storage space is used vs free. These are completely separate resources.
